127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:

136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137

29	### Decision 1: group-law substrate — coherent monoidal category vs. existence-of-iso
30	
31	- **Mathlib idiom**: `CommRing.Pic R := Shrink (Skeleton (SemimoduleCat R))ˣ`
32	  (`Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean:407-412`). The `CommGroup` comes from
33	  `(…)ˣ` (units) of `Skeleton.instCommMonoid`, which is
34	  `commMonoidOfSkeletalBraided` over `monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal`
35	  (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean:38-47, 80-81`). **The monoid
36	  laws are literally:**
37	  ```
38	  one_mul  X     := hC ⟨λ_ X⟩
39	  mul_one  X     := hC ⟨ρ_ X⟩
40	  mul_assoc X Y Z := hC ⟨α_ X Y Z⟩      -- hC : Skeletal C, i.e. (X ≅ Y) → X = Y
41	  mul_comm  X Y  := hC ⟨β_ X Y⟩
42	  ```
43	  Each axiom is discharged by feeding ONE iso (`⟨α_ X Y Z⟩`, a `Nonempty`) to
44	  `Skeletal`. The pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherence carried by the
45	  `MonoidalCategory` typeclass is **never invoked** in any monoid-law proof.

47	  existence of the associator/unitor/braiding isos. (It gets them "for free"
48	  from the bundled `MonoidalCategory ModuleCat` instance, but the laws don't
49	  touch coherence.)

51	  locally-trivial modules from four existence-of-iso lemmas (`tensorObj_assoc_iso`,
52	  `tensorObj_left/right_unitor`, `tensorObj_braiding`, `exists_tensorObj_inverse`),

57	  `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance — whose pentagon fields would require

70	- **Mathlib idiom**: the only thing `monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` ever needs from
71	  the associator is `⟨α_ X Y Z⟩` — a single iso. It builds NO localizer
72	  monoidality, NO `(J.W).IsMonoidal`, NO stalkwise-tensor commutation. The
73	  whole `(J.W).IsMonoidal`/whiskering apparatus has no counterpart in the
74	  Mathlib Picard pipeline.
75	- **Project's current path**: `tensorObj_assoc_iso` is built via ROUTE (d):
76	  transport the global presheaf associator `α` through sheafification, using
77	  `W_whiskerLeft/Right_of_W` to pull the inner sheafification out of the nested
78	  `tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P`. Those bottom out in
79	  `isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W` (open sorry), whose two residuals are
80	  the Mathlib-absent d.1/d.2 stalk-⊗-over-a-varying-ring machinery (~300 LOC of
81	  apparatus L301-601 hangs off this).
82	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. The whiskering route is an over-engineered way
83	  to obtain a datum (`Nonempty((M⊗N)⊗P ≅ M⊗(N⊗P))`) that a lighter route
84	  supplies. For **locally-trivial** M,N,P (the only case the group law touches)
85	  the associator is obtainable from `tensorObj_restrict_iso` alone — the same
86	  single ingredient `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`, the unitors, the inverse, and
87	  the bifunctor all already funnel through. Closing `tensorObj_restrict_iso`
88	  (H1: presheaf-level `pushforwardPushforwardAdj` / sectionwise extension of
89	  scalars) thus retires the *entire* whiskering+stalk apparatus as dead code.
90	- **Cost of divergence (current route)**: a 6-iter-stuck sorry plus ~300 LOC of
91	  whiskering/stalk scaffolding, whose terminal dependency (d.2 stalk-⊗ over the
92	  varying ring) is the project's own "genuinely Mathlib-absent (largest piece)"
93	  (L543-544). All of it avoidable.
94	- **CAVEAT the planner must budget (do not under-scope)**: "build the associator
95	  directly by gluing, mirroring `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`" understates the
96	  work. `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial` proves a **Prop** — `∀ x, ∃ U, Nonempty(…≅𝒪_U)`
97	  — purely pointwise, with NO overlap compatibility. The associator is **global
98	  data**: a single iso of sheaves on all of X. You cannot assemble it from
99	  pointwise-chosen trivialization isos (arbitrary local isos do not agree on
100	  overlaps). The correct construction:
101	  on each open `Uᵢ` of a common trivialising cover, form the **canonical**
102	  ```
103	  ((M⊗N)⊗P)|Uᵢ ≅ (M|⊗N|)⊗P|  --[restrict_iso, twice]
104	              ≅ M|⊗(N|⊗P|)     --[canonical PRESHEAF associator on restrictions]
105	              ≅ (M⊗(N⊗P))|Uᵢ  --[restrict_iso, twice]
106	  ```
107	  Because `tensorObj_restrict_iso` is natural and the presheaf associator is
108	  natural/canonical, these local isos **do** agree on overlaps, so a
109	  "Hom-of-sheaves-is-a-sheaf" gluing assembles the global iso. This needs:
110	  (i) `tensorObj_restrict_iso` (the named funnel), (ii) the canonical presheaf
111	  associator (`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator`, already
112	  available — it is Step 2 of the current route), and (iii) a gluing-of-isos
113	  lemma + the overlap-naturality proof. Item (iii) is a real, bounded cost — but
114	  it does NOT touch `(J.W).IsMonoidal`, d.1, or d.2. (One could even skip the
115	  cover entirely and glue the canonical local isos directly via the sheaf
116	  condition on internal Hom, but the cover form matches the in-file template.)
117	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — drop the whiskering/`(J.W).IsMonoidal`/
118	  stalk route; build `tensorObj_assoc_iso` from `tensorObj_restrict_iso`

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, the entire monoidal scaffolding for (3) is already
88	available abstractly, and the project's task is to \emph{instantiate} it rather
89	than to hand-assemble the coherence data. Two facts drive this. First, the
90	category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a
91	presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric) monoidal category in
92	Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)), with the sectionwise
93	relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\); this applies
94	verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second,
95	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\) --- associator,
100	left/right unitors, braiding, and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
101	naturalities) derived. Sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the
102	localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class
103	\(W = J.W\), so the monoidal structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is
104	\emph{free} from this API once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is supplied.
105	
106	Consequently the only genuinely-new obligation is the single instance
107	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) for the sheafification localizer on
108	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\). Its two fields are the
109	left/right whiskering-stability statements \(J.W\,g \Rightarrow J.W(F \triangleleft
110	g)\) and \(J.W\,g \Rightarrow J.W(g \triangleright F)\) for an \emph{arbitrary}
111	module \(F\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), and these are flatness-free: a
112	\(J.W\)-morphism is a stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x =
113	\mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an isomorphism (tensoring with an
114	isomorphism preserves isomorphisms --- no flatness, no local triviality). The
115	load-bearing half is the local-injectivity statement
116	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, whose stalkwise proof needs two
117	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)-level ingredients that are Mathlib-absent at the
118	pinned commit (the module-level stalk characterisation of \(J.W\), and the
119	commutation of the stalk with the relative tensor); this is the
120	\emph{sole remaining open obligation} for the whole group-law engine.
121	
122	With \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) in hand, the substrate \(\otimes_X\) carries the
123	associator, unitors and braiding from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), and the
124	group law assembles the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units
125	group is the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), exactly as
126	Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) as
127	\(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\). The
128	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
129	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse
130	existentially. Under route~(e), once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is in hand the
131	geometric compatibility isomorphism \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)
132	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) is not needed for that path (the coherence
133	comes from the API); under the PRIMARY locally-trivial route, by contrast, it is
134	on the critical path (see \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, PRIMARY).
135

285	\leanok
286	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
287	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
289	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For

370	\begin{remark}
371	[The full monoidal category is cheap via route~(e); the group law consumes only propositions]
372	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
373	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
374	        lem:whisker_of_W}
375	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
376	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
377	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
378	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
379	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
380	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. Under route~(e) this is
381	  \emph{cheap}, not expensive: it is exactly the output of
382	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
383	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
384	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), once the single instance
385	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) is supplied. All the
386	  coherence is then derived by the API.
387	
388	  Crucially, \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) needs \emph{no} closed structure. Its
389	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
390	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
391	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
392	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
393	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\) (tensoring with an isomorphism
394	  preserves isomorphisms). This is the same technique Mathlib uses for
395	  fixed-base presheaves in
396	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\), ported to
397	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). It uses neither
398	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
399	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
400	  false over a non-affine open and is therefore off the critical path); the
401	  earlier reading that arbitrary-\(F\) whisker stability requires
402	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) was the \emph{wrong altitude}.
403	
404	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
405	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
406	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
407	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
408	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
409	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
410	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
411	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
412	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
413	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, all now supplied by the route~(e) monoidal structure;
414	  under route~(e) none of them is built from the restriction-compatibility
415	  isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which is instead on the critical
416	  path of the PRIMARY locally-trivial route, via
417	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
418	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
419	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
420	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\): there
421	  over a fixed ring, here over the sheafification localizer via
422	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\).
423	\end{remark}

689	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
690	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
691	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
692	  \[
693	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
694	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
695	  \]
696	
697	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
698	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
699	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
700	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
701	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
702	
703	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
704	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
705	  linear maps (Mathlib
706	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
707	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
708	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
709	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
710	
711	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
712	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
713	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
714	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
715	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
716	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
717	\end{proof}

787	dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}; it uses \emph{no}

887	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
888	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
889	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
890	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
891	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
892	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
893	  On such a \(V\):
894	  \begin{itemize}
895	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
896	      gives a canonical isomorphism

899	      \]

904	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
905	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong

912	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
913	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
914	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
915	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level

934	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving

  \[
    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
  \]
  in which:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
  \end{itemize}
  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
  composites agree on the overlap.

  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
  local inverses produces a global morphism
  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
  associator.

  The single non-formal input is the restriction-compatibility comparison
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf associator
  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules internal-hom gluing
  are already available. Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
  are not consumed.
\end{proof}

  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left

  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
  \otimes_S N)\). This was the former ``H2'' bottom gap.

  \[
    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
  \]
  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (``H1''). One exhibits a
  presheaf-level adjunction
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
  composing the resulting comparison with
  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.

  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
  presheaf level. The depth of this sub-chain is \emph{uncertain} and may span

  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law: the
  direct gluing construction of the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
  invokes it (twice per cover member) to descend to the canonical presheaf-level

\begin{lemma}
[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
  product: the canonical map
  \[
    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
  \]
  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient (formerly
  ``H2'') of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
  adjunction (``H1'') as the sole open residual there.
\end{lemma}
